content.text: Shared Cost Pool Rules for Platform Overhead

Not every Claude API dollar can be attributed directly to a single team. Platform services, shared gateways, security evaluations, and untagged calls create a residual cost pool that must be distributed across consuming teams through explicit rules rather than direct attribution. The way you define these rules determines whether downstream chargeback is perceived as fair or arbitrary.

This document covers how to classify costs into direct, shared, and overhead pools, the allocation rules for each pool type, the mechanics of implementing those rules, and the governance process for keeping pool definitions and rules current.

attrs.alt: Shared Cost Pool Taxonomy and Allocation Rules

content.text: Every Claude API dollar should be classified into exactly one of three pool types at the time the allocation report is generated:
One and only one team generated the cost; tag is present and correct
100% to the tagged cost center; no split
Multiple teams use the same deployment or API key; costs are interleaved by design
Split by consumption weight, fixed weight agreement, or usage metric
Platform operations, security, testing, monitoring, untagged production calls
Absorbed by platform budget, equally split, or headcount-weighted split
< 5% (mature program); up to 15% for early programs

The overhead pool percentage is a proxy for tagging discipline. A program where 15% of spend lands in overhead has a tagging problem — not a cost problem. Track overhead percentage as a first-class FinOps KPI and set a quarterly reduction target.
When multiple teams consume from a shared deployment, three split methods are available:
Each team's share equals their fraction of total tokens consumed from the shared deployment during the period. This is the most accurate method because it ties cost to actual usage.

import pandas as pd

def token_weighted_split(shared_ledger_df: pd.DataFrame, pool_cost_usd: float) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """
    Split a shared cost pool by each team's fraction of total tokens.
    Input tokens + output tokens form the weight basis.
    Cache reads count at 10% weight (they cost ~10% of input tokens).
    """
    df = shared_ledger_df.copy()
    df["weighted_tokens"] = (
        df["input_tokens"]
        + df["output_tokens"]
        + df.get("cache_read_tokens", 0) * 0.1
    )

    team_weights = df.groupby("team")["weighted_tokens"].sum().reset_index()
    total = team_weights["weighted_tokens"].sum()

    if total == 0:
        return pd.DataFrame()

    team_weights["share"] = team_weights["weighted_tokens"] / total
    team_weights["allocated_cost_usd"] = (team_weights["share"] * pool_cost_usd).round(4)

    # Rounding correction: assign residual to largest share
    residual = round(pool_cost_usd - team_weights["allocated_cost_usd"].sum(), 4)
    if residual != 0:
        idx = team_weights["share"].idxmax()
        team_weights.loc[idx, "allocated_cost_usd"] += residual

    return team_weights[["team", "weighted_tokens", "share", "allocated_cost_usd"]]

Teams negotiate a fixed percentage split at the start of the fiscal year and that split holds regardless of actual consumption. Useful when consumption is expected to be roughly equal or when one team sponsors another team's usage.

Split by a business metric that correlates with value derived — for example, number of active users per team, number of production API endpoints served, or number of customer accounts. Use this when token consumption does not reflect business value (e.g., one team's use case is inherently token-heavy but lower-value).

The overhead pool covers costs that cannot be attributed to any consuming team: platform operations, security evaluations, quality monitoring, and the residual untagged production calls after cleanup efforts.
API gateway health checks, integration tests, CI pipeline calls
Absorb in platform budget — these benefit all teams equally and are low cost
Red-team calls, adversarial probes, content filter testing
Absorb in security budget — not attributable to a consuming team
Automated evaluation harness runs, A/B quality checks
Equal split across all active teams — all teams benefit from quality assurance
Calls with missing or invalid cost_center tag
Equal split across all teams — creates incentive to fix tagging (all teams pay for the gap)
New model version testing before rollout
Absorb in platform budget — infrastructure investment, not team usage

Publishing that untagged costs are split equally across all teams — including teams that tag correctly — is the most effective incentive to fix tagging quickly. Teams that tag well will pressure teams that don't.

Cost pool rules are not set-once. As new teams onboard, as platform services evolve, and as untagged spend patterns change, the rules need to be reviewed and updated. Governance prevents rules from drifting silently.
Review pool composition: what percentage of spend fell into each pool type this quarter?
Review shared pool membership: are the right teams in each shared pool? Have new teams started consuming from shared services?
Review overhead pool categories: are any overhead categories growing as a percentage of total spend? Investigate root causes.
Update fixed weight agreements if consumption patterns have shifted more than ±10 percentage points from agreed weights
Publish the updated pool rules before the next billing cycle begins

Changes to cost pool rules that affect chargeback amounts must be communicated at least 30 days before the cycle in which they take effect. Retroactive application of new rules is not permitted without explicit agreement from all affected teams.
Adding a new team to a shared pool
Moving a cost category from overhead to shared

import pandas as pd

def full_monthly_allocation(
    ledger_df: pd.DataFrame,
    shared_pool_definitions: dict,   # pool_name -> list of team names in pool
    overhead_categories: list[str],  # list of use_case values treated as overhead
    active_teams: list[str],
) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """
    Produce the complete monthly allocation table combining direct,
    shared, and overhead pool results.
    """
    rows = []

    # 1. Direct attribution — tagged, non-overhead calls
    direct = ledger_df[
        ledger_df["cost_center"].notna()
        & ~ledger_df["use_case"].isin(overhead_categories)
        & ~ledger_df["team"].apply(
            lambda t: any(t in pool for pool in shared_pool_definitions.values())
        )
    ]
    for (team, cc), grp in direct.groupby(["team", "cost_center"]):
        rows.append({"team": team, "cost_center": cc, "pool_type": "direct",
                     "allocated_cost_usd": round(grp["cost_usd"].sum(), 4)})

    # 2. Shared pools — one split per defined pool
    for pool_name, pool_teams in shared_pool_definitions.items():
        pool_data = ledger_df[ledger_df["team"].isin(pool_teams)]
        pool_cost = pool_data["cost_usd"].sum()
        split = token_weighted_split(pool_data, pool_cost)
        for _, row in split.iterrows():
            rows.append({"team": row["team"], "cost_center": None,
                         "pool_type": f"shared:{pool_name}",
                         "allocated_cost_usd": row["allocated_cost_usd"]})

    # 3. Overhead — equal split of untagged + overhead use-cases
    overhead = ledger_df[
        ledger_df["cost_center"].isna()
        | ledger_df["use_case"].isin(overhead_categories)
    ]
    overhead_cost = overhead["cost_usd"].sum()
    per_team_overhead = round(overhead_cost / len(active_teams), 4) if active_teams else 0
    for team in active_teams:
        rows.append({"team": team, "cost_center": None,
                     "pool_type": "overhead", "allocated_cost_usd": per_team_overhead})

    result = pd.DataFrame(rows)
    return result.groupby(["team", "pool_type"])["allocated_cost_usd"].sum().reset_index()

Every allocation report should reconcile to the actual Anthropic invoice — the sum of all allocated amounts (direct + shared + overhead) must equal the invoice total for the period. A gap between allocation total and invoice indicates missing ledger records, pricing calculation errors, or credits not reflected in the ledger.

def reconcile_to_invoice(
    allocation_df,          # output of full_monthly_allocation()
    invoice_total_usd: float,
    tolerance_usd: float = 1.0,   # allow rounding up to $1
) -> dict:
    allocated_total = allocation_df["allocated_cost_usd"].sum()
    variance = abs(allocated_total - invoice_total_usd)
    passed = variance <= tolerance_usd
    return {
        "invoice_total_usd": invoice_total_usd,
        "allocated_total_usd": round(allocated_total, 4),
        "variance_usd": round(variance, 4),
        "reconciled": passed,
        "status": "OK" if passed else f"FAIL — variance ${variance:.4f} exceeds tolerance ${tolerance_usd}",
    }

# Usage
result = reconcile_to_invoice(allocation_df, invoice_total_usd=14_382.17)
if not result["reconciled"]:
    raise ValueError(f"Allocation does not reconcile: {result['status']}")
print(f"Reconciled: ${result['allocated_total_usd']:,.2f} = ${result['invoice_total_usd']:,.2f}")
Ledger missing records (network timeout, write failure)
Compare ledger row count to Anthropic usage API; backfill missing records
Duplicate records in ledger; pricing rate above actual
Deduplicate by call_id; verify pricing constants against current Anthropic pricing page
Large untagged spend; platform evaluation spike
Investigate untagged calls by call_id; check for runaway evaluation jobs
Team onboarded or offboarded; use-case reclassification
Verify team membership changes; check for shared key reassignment

Pool rules must be documented in a versioned policy document — not just in code. Finance, legal, and auditors need to be able to understand the methodology without reading Python. The policy document should include:
Each policy version is date-stamped; prior versions retained for audit trail
Explicit list of which API keys, services, and use_case tags belong to each pool
Which method (token-weighted, fixed-weight, headcount) applies to each shared pool, with the agreed parameters
Which categories are overhead, and whether they are absorbed by platform budget or split across teams
When the policy is reviewed and who approves changes
Who adjudicates disputes about pool membership or split amounts

A cost pool policy that only exists in code will be challenged by every team that sees an unexpected charge. Maintain a plain-language version in your governance wiki and link to it from every chargeback report.

Shared cost pool rules convert the ambiguous 'platform overhead' bucket into an explicit, documented, auditable allocation policy. Classify every Claude API dollar as direct, shared, or overhead; apply the appropriate split rule to each pool; and govern the rules through a quarterly review process with advance notice requirements for changes. The overhead pool percentage is the key health metric — drive it below 5% through improved tagging, then maintain it there. Teams that understand exactly how their chargeback was calculated are far more likely to accept it and act on it.
